topics.title: Reactive Programming (Project Reactor)
topics.description: Reactive programming is a non-blocking, event-driven programming paradigm based on the Reactive Streams specification. Project Reactor (used in Spring WebFlux) provides two core types: Mono<T> (0 or 1 element) and Flux<T> (0 to N elements). Nothing executes until you subscribe. Operators transform the pipeline lazily. Ideal for I/O-heavy microservices where you need to serve thousands of concurrent requests without blocking threads.
topics.story: Think of traditional blocking code as a restaurant where each waiter (thread) is stuck at the table waiting for the chef (DB/API) to finish cooking before they can serve another customer. The waiter does nothing useful while waiting — they just stand there. Reactive programming is like a pager system: the waiter takes the order, gives the customer a pager, and immediately goes to serve the next table. When the kitchen is done, the pager buzzes (event fires) and the waiter delivers the food. One waiter serves 100 tables because they're never idle.
topics.analogy: Mono<T> = Promise/Optional — at most one value. Flux<T> = Stream/Observable — zero to many values. subscribe() = attach the pager. map() = transform each item. flatMap() = transform + merge async results. filter() = skip items. zip() = combine two streams element by element. block() = waiter stands and waits (defeats the purpose — avoid in prod). Backpressure = kitchen tells waiter 'slow down, I'm full'.
topics.memoryAnchor: Mono.just(x) — wrap single value. Mono.empty() — no value. Flux.just(a,b,c) — wrap multiple values. Flux.fromIterable(list). .map(fn) — sync transform. .flatMap(fn) — async transform (fn returns Mono/Flux). .filter(pred). .subscribe(onNext, onError, onComplete). .block() — synchronous wait (testing only). .doOnNext(log) — side effect without transforming. .zipWith(other) — combine two Monos. .collectList() — Flux → Mono<List>. .delayElements(Duration) — add latency.
topics.firstPrinciples: Reactive Streams spec defines 4 interfaces: Publisher<T>, Subscriber<T>, Subscription, Processor. Publisher produces data, Subscriber consumes it, Subscription controls demand (backpressure). Nothing happens until `subscribe()` — the pipeline is assembled lazily like a query builder. Each operator wraps the upstream in a new Publisher. When subscribe() is called, it propagates upstream, triggering onSubscribe → onNext (N times) → onComplete or onError. Backpressure: Subscriber can request N items at a time, preventing memory overflow from a fast producer.
topics.bruteForce: Blocking code: String result = dbService.findUser(id); // blocks thread for 50ms. With 200 threads and 1000 requests: 800 requests queue up waiting for a free thread. Throughput ceiling = threadPoolSize / averageLatencySeconds.
topics.optimizedApproach: Mono<String> result = reactiveDbService.findUser(id); // returns immediately. No thread blocking. result.flatMap(user -> externalApi.fetch(user.id())).subscribe(...). Event loop (default 2×CPU threads) handles thousands of concurrent operations. Throughput ceiling is I/O bandwidth, not thread count.
topics.whenToUse: Spring WebFlux APIs with high I/O concurrency. Aggregating multiple microservice calls in parallel with zip(). Streaming large datasets (Flux) to clients. NOT for: CPU-bound tasks (still blocks), simple CRUD with Spring MVC (adds complexity without benefit), teams new to reactive (steep learning curve). Rule of thumb: reactive all the way down — mixing blocking and reactive kills performance.

examples.title: Mono and Flux Core Operations
examples.explanation: Mono.just() and Flux.just() create publishers. Nothing executes until subscribe() is called. filter/map/flatMap are lazy operators — they build the pipeline. flatMap handles async operations that return Mono/Flux. block() is synchronous and defeats reactive purpose — use only in tests or main(). zipWith() combines two Monos into one. onErrorReturn() provides a fallback on error.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring WebFlux controller: `@GetMapping("/user/{id}") public Mono<UserResponse> getUser(@PathVariable Long id) { return userRepo.findById(id).flatMap(u -> roleService.getRoles(u.id()).map(roles -> new UserResponse(u, roles))).switchIfEmpty(Mono.error(new NotFoundException())); }` — no blocking, handles thousands of concurrent requests with event-loop threads.

problems.title: Transform and Filter a Flux
problems.description:
Given a list of integers read from stdin (space-separated on one line), create a Flux from them, filter out odd numbers, square each remaining number, and print each result on a new line.

Sample input: `1 2 3 4 5 6`
Expected output:
```
4
16
36
```
problems.hint1: Use Flux.fromArray() or Flux.just() with the parsed integers.
problems.hint3: Use .subscribe(System.out::println) to print each item. Call .blockLast() after subscribe to wait for completion in main().
problems.editorial: Flux.just(array) creates a publisher from the array. filter() uses a predicate — passes only even numbers. map() transforms synchronously. subscribe(System.out::println) triggers execution and prints each item. Since Flux.just() is synchronous (all items already in memory), there's no need to block — the pipeline runs inline on the current thread.

problems.title: Combine Two Monos with ZipWith
problems.description:
Read two lines from stdin: a first name and a last name. Use `Mono.just()` for each, then `zipWith()` to combine them into `"LastName, FirstName"`. Print the result.

Sample input:
```
John
Doe
```
Expected: `Doe, John`
problems.hint1: Create two Mono<String> from the two input lines.
problems.hint3: Use .block() to get the value and print it, or subscribe with a print consumer.
problems.editorial: zipWith() combines two publishers element-by-element. Both Monos emit exactly one value, so the combinator function runs once producing one output. The result is a new Mono<String>. Subscribe triggers execution. zipWith() is commonly used in reactive microservices to combine results from two parallel async calls.

problems.title: Error Handling with onErrorReturn
problems.description:
Read N integers from stdin (one per line). For each integer:
- If it's positive, emit its square root (formatted to 2 decimal places)
- If it's zero or negative, the sqrt would fail — use `onErrorReturn("ERR")` to handle `ArithmeticException`

Create a `Mono<String>` per line using `Mono.fromCallable(() -> safeSqrt(n))` that throws `ArithmeticException` for n <= 0.

Sample input:
```
4
-1
9
0
```
Expected:
```
2.00
ERR
3.00
ERR
```
problems.hint1: Use Mono.fromCallable(() -> { if (n <= 0) throw new ArithmeticException(); return String.format("%.2f", Math.sqrt(n)); }).
problems.hint2: Chain .onErrorReturn("ERR") to catch the exception and return the fallback string.
problems.hint3: Call .block() or .subscribe(System.out::println) on each Mono inside the loop.
problems.editorial: Mono.fromCallable() wraps a checked-exception-capable supplier. When the exception is thrown inside, the Mono enters error state. onErrorReturn() intercepts the error signal and replaces it with a fallback value, converting an error Mono into a success Mono. This is the reactive equivalent of try-catch with a default value.
